gene_description: Nuclear Receptor Subfamily 1 Group D Member 1 (REV-ERBα) — heme-binding nuclear receptor that represses BMAL1 transcription, forming a critical link between the circadian clock and metabolic regulation (glucose/lipid homeostasis).

source.title: Associations between polymorphisms in the NR1D1 gene encoding for nuclear receptor REV-ERBα and circadian typologies
source.finding: NR1D1 rs12941497 genotypes are significantly associated with chronotype classification (morning/intermediate/evening types), supporting REV-ERBα's role in circadian rhythm regulation and its downstream metabolic effects.

CC.effect: Homozygous C carriers show stronger association with evening chronotype. Altered REV-ERBα-mediated circadian repression may shift metabolic timing, affecting glucose and lipid homeostasis. Evening chronotype is associated with later meal timing, increased metabolic syndrome risk, and impaired glucose tolerance.
CC.recommendation: Align meal timing with circadian biology — avoid late-night eating. Try to maintain consistent sleep-wake schedule. Morning light exposure to help entrain circadian clock. Time-restricted feeding (earlier eating window) may counteract evening chronotype metabolic effects.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate chronotype tendency. Standard circadian metabolic regulation with minor variations.
CT.recommendation: Maintain regular sleep schedule. Standard meal timing recommendations.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate chronotype tendency. Standard circadian metabolic regulation with minor variations.
TC.recommendation: Maintain regular sleep schedule. Standard meal timing recommendations.

TT.effect: Homozygous T carriers show stronger association with morning chronotype. REV-ERBα circadian repression cycle is well-aligned with standard metabolic timing. Generally favorable metabolic profile associated with morning chronotype.
TT.recommendation: Natural circadian alignment is favorable. Maintain regular schedule to preserve this advantage.
